cubic meter] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the term "volumetric enzyme productivity" (Qp) and state its units. Why is it considered a more reliable design metric than final enzyme titer alone?** **Model Answer:** Volumetric enzyme productivity is the amount of enzyme produced per unit working volume per unit time. $$ Q_{p} = \dfrac{P_{f} - P_{0}}{t_{cycle} \cdot V_{w}} \quad [\text{U/L·h or g/L·h}] $$ It is more reliable than final titer because: 1. It accounts for **time efficiency** of the cycle (including downtime for cleaning, sterilization, harvesting). 2. It directly determines **reactor volume** and **number of reactors** for a target annual production. 3. It reveals cases where high titer but long cycle time gives lower true productivity than a faster, lower-titer process. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain why "P/V" (power per unit volume) is the most commonly used scale-up criterion for aerobic enzyme bioreactors.** **Model Answer:** $P/V$ (gassed power per unit liquid volume) is the preferred scale-up criterion because: 1. It correlates directly with **$k_{L}a$** (oxygen transfer coefficient), which is the rate-limiting step in most aerobic enzyme fermentations. 2. It captures both **mixing intensity** and **turbulence dissipation** at the impeller. 3. It is **measurable at both lab and plant scale** using torque meters on the agitator shaft. 4. It implicitly controls mean energy dissipation rate $\bar{\varepsilon}$, which governs cell–bubble interactions. Limitation: it does not preserve **tip speed** or **shear**, so for shear-sensitive systems, multi-criterion scale-up (constant $P/V$ **and** constant $v_{tip}$) must be applied. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the steady-state expression for enzyme (product) concentration in a CSTR operating under Monod growth kinetics and Luedeking–Piret enzyme production. Discuss why fed-batch outperforms CSTR for the production of non-growth-associated enzymes. **(b) [7 Marks)** A pilot bioreactor of $V = 100\ L$ is used to produce an extracellular protease by *Bacillus subtilis*. The following data are obtained: - $\mu_{max} = 0.45\ h^{-1}$, $K_{s} = 0.2\ g/L$ - $Y_{x/s} = 0.4\ g/g$, $S_{0} = 25\ g/L$ - $q_{O_2} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}\ gO_{2}/gDW \cdot h$ - $X_{max} = 8\ g/L$ - $C^{*} = 7.5\ mg/L$, target $C_{L} = 2.5\ mg/L$ Calculate: (i) The maximum oxygen uptake rate (OUR) at peak biomass. (ii) The minimum $k_{L}a$ required. (iii) Comment on the suitability of a standard Rushton turbine STR for this system. **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Mass balance for biomass in CSTR at steady state: $$ 0 = F X_{0} - F X + \mu X V $$ Since $X_{0} = 0$: $$ D X = \mu X \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \mu $$ Applying Monod: $$ D = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_{s} + S} $$ Solving for steady-state substrate: $$ S = \dfrac{K_{s} \cdot D}{\mu_{max} - D} $$ Steady-state biomass: $$ X = Y_{x/s} \cdot (S_{0} - S) = Y_{x/s} \cdot \left( S_{0} - \dfrac{K_{s} D}{\mu_{max}-D} \right) $$ Mass balance for product (Luedeking–Piret): $$ 0 = -F P + V (\alpha \mu X + \beta X) $$ $$ P = \dfrac{V}{F} (\alpha \mu + \beta) X = \tau X (\alpha \mu + \beta) $$ For purely non-growth-associated product ($\alpha = 0$): $$ P = \beta X \tau = \dfrac{\beta Y_{x/s} S_{0} \tau}{\dfrac{K_{s}}{\mu_{max}} + \tau} \quad \text{(after substituting } S \to 0 \text{ at long } \tau \text{)} $$ Discussion: In fed-batch, $\tau \to \infty$ effectively (no outlet), so $P$ keeps rising with time as biomass accumulates. In CSTR, $\tau$ is fixed and bounded by $1/D_{c}$; beyond this, washout occurs. Hence fed-batch achieves 3–10× higher titers. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) OUR at peak biomass:** $$ \begin{aligned} OUR &= q_{O_2} \cdot X_{max} \\ &= 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \\ &= 1.44 \times 10^{-2}\ gO_{2}/L \cdot h \\ &= 14.4\ mgO_{2}/L \cdot h \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Minimum $k_{L}a$:** $$ \begin{aligned} k_{L}a_{min} &= \dfrac{OUR}{C^{*} - C_{L}} \\ &= \dfrac{14.4}{7.5 - 2.5} \\ &= \dfrac{14.4}{5.0} \\ &= 2.88\ \text{h}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to s⁻¹: $k_{L}a = 2.88/3600 = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}^{-1}$. **(iii) Suitability:** A standard Rushton turbine STR is **suitable** because: - *Bacillus subtilis* is a rod-shaped, robust bacterium tolerating shear up to $v_{tip} \approx 3\ m/s$. - The required $k_{L}a = 2.88\ h^{-1}$ is easily achieved even with single Rushton at $P/V \approx 500\ W/m^{3}$ (typical achievable $k_{L}a \approx 100–300\ h^{-1}$). - The viscosity of the broth remains low (Newtonian), so Rushton turbines operate efficiently without cavity flooding at these aeration rates. --- #### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] (Alternative Choice)** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled schematic, describe the design features of a standard stirred-tank bioreactor (STR) for aerobic enzyme production. Explain how each feature contributes to optimal enzyme yield. **(b) [7 Marks]** An industrial enzyme plant uses a fed-batch STR of working volume 50 m³ for producing an industrial protease. - Initial charge: $V_{0} = 20\ m^{3}$, $X_{0} = 1\ g/L$, $S_{0} = 30\ g/L$ (batch substrate) - Exponential feed: $F(t) = F_{0} \cdot e^{\mu_{set} \cdot t}$ with $F_{0} = 50\ L/h$, $\mu_{set} = 0.2\ h^{-1}$ - $\mu_{max} = 0.3\ h^{-1}$, $Y_{x/s} = 0.45\ g/g$ - Assume substrate in reactor remains at limiting level (≈ 0.2 g/L) throughout feeding. Calculate: (i) The biomass concentration $X$ at $t = 15\ h$ assuming $100\%$ biomass yield on fed substrate. (ii) The total volume at $t = 15\ h$. (iii) The total enzyme productivity assuming $\beta = 0.05\ U/gDW \cdot h$ and the run is harvested at 15 h. **Model Solution:** **(a) Schematic Description (7 marks):** [Student should draw STR showing]: 1. **Cylindrical vessel with dished top and bottom** — pressure-rated for SIP at 121 °C. 2. **Aspect ratio H/D = 2:1 to 3:1** — provides adequate hydrostatic head for oxygen dissolution. 3. **Multiple Rushton or pitched-blade impellers (2–4)** on a single shaft — adequate for 10–200 m³ volumes; the lower one for gas dispersion, the upper for bulk mixing. 4. **Ring or spider sparger** below the lower impeller — for bubble formation. 5. **Four radial baffles (W = D/10)** — prevent vortex and improve mixing. 6. **Cooling jacket and/or internal coil** — remove metabolic heat (typically 5–15 kW/m³ for industrial aerobic fermentations). 7. **Probes**: pH, DO, temperature, foam, level — for closed-loop control. 8. **Sampling and harvest valves** — aseptic design with steam blocks. 9. **CIP spray ball** — for clean-in-place. 10. **Top-headspace vent with 0.2 µm filter** — for off-gas. **Each feature contributes to optimal yield** as summarized in the table below: | Feature | Contribution to Optimal Yield | |---------|------------------------------| | High H/D ratio | Long residence of bubbles → high kLa | | Multiple impellers | Avoids vertical stratification of substrate | | Baffles | Eliminate vortex, enhance turbulence | | Cooling coil | Prevents thermal kill of cells | | DO probe | Avoids oxygen limitation | | Aseptic design | Prevents contamination → long productive phase | **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Biomass at $t = 15$ h:** Total substrate fed in exponential feed (assuming $S_{in} = 400\ g/L$ — typical industrial glucose feed): Volume fed: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{fed}(t) &= \int_{0}^{t} F_{0} \, e^{\mu_{set} \cdot t'} \, dt' \\ &= \dfrac{F_{0}}{\mu_{set}} \left( e^{\mu_{set} t} - 1 \right) \\ &= \dfrac{50}{0.2} \left( e^{0.2 \times 15} - 1 \right) \\ &= 250 \times (e^{3} - 1) \\ &= 250 \times (20.09 - 1) \\ &= 250 \times 19.09 \\ &= 4772\ L \approx 4.77\ m^{3} \end{aligned} $$ Substrate consumed from feed: $S_{added} = 4772 \times 0.4 = 1908\ kg$ (assuming $S_{feed} = 400\ g/L = 400\ kg/m^3$, then $4.77\ m^3 \times 400 = 1908\ kg$). Substrate consumed from initial batch: $(30 - 0.2)\ g/L \times 20\ m^3 = 596\ kg$. Biomass formed from fed substrate: $1908 \times 0.45 = 858.6\ kg$. Biomass from initial: $596 \times 0.45 = 268.2\ kg$. Total biomass: $858.6 + 268.2 + X_0 V_0 = 1126.8 + 20 = 1146.8\ kg$. **(ii) Total volume at 15 h:** $V_{total} = V_{0} + V_{fed} = 20 + 4.77 = 24.77\ m^{3}$. **(iii) Total enzyme productivity:** Total enzyme produced: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{total} &= \beta \cdot X_{avg} \cdot V_{avg} \cdot t \\ &\approx \beta \cdot X_{final} \cdot V_{final} \cdot t \\ &= 0.05 \times (1146.8 / 24.77) \times 24.77 \times 15 \\ &= 0.05 \times 46.30 \times 24.77 \times 15 \\ &\approx 0.05 \times 1146.8 \times 15 \\ &\approx 860.1\ \text{U} \end{aligned} $$ Volumetric productivity: $$ Q_{p} = \dfrac{860.1}{24.77 \times 15} \approx 2.32\ \text{U/L·h} $$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Bioreactor Design Problems:** > 1. **Unit mismatch** — Students forget to convert $k_{L}a$ from h⁻¹ to s⁻¹ (or vice versa). Always verify units before final numerical answer. > 2. **Ignoring washout** — When asked for "maximum productivity dilution rate," students compute $D_{opt}$ without first checking that $D_{opt} < D_{c}$. Always derive the washout condition explicitly. > 3. **Confusing $k_{L}a$ with mass transfer coefficient $k_{L}$** — $k_{L}a$ is the **volumetric** coefficient (units h⁻¹ or s⁻¹), while $k_{L}$ alone is the interfacial coefficient (units m/s). Marks are deducted when these are interchanged. > 4. **Forgetting sterile boundary conditions** — In a CSTR problem, if you forget $X_{0} = 0$ and $P_{0} = 0$, the entire derivation becomes inconsistent. > 5. **Numerical rounding** — Do not round intermediate steps. Carry at least 4 significant figures until the final step. > 6. **Forgetting temperature dependence** — $C^{*}$ is a strong function of temperature (≈ 7.5 mg/L at 30 °C vs 6.2 mg/L at 37 °C). Always state the temperature. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Bioreactor Design for Enzyme Production:** - ☐ Bioreactor design objective: **maximize $Q_{p}$**, not just $P_{final}$. - ☐ Three design layers: **Kinetics → Mass Transfer → Hydrodynamics → Scale-Up**. - ☐ **Luedeking–Piret** is the universal rate law for microbially-produced enzymes. - ☐ **Fed-batch** is the industrial default for non-growth-associated enzymes. - ☐ Critical parameters to control: **DO (>30 %), pH (±0.2), T (±0.5 °C), shear ($v_{tip}$ ≤ 1.5 m/s for sensitive cells)**. - ☐ **$k_{L}a$** is the master mass transfer parameter; required = $OUR / (C^* - C_L)$. - ☐ **Scale-up criteria** (in order of frequency): constant $P/V$ > constant $k_{L}a$ > constant $v_{tip}$ > constant mixing time > constant shear. - ☐ Geometric similarity ratio: $R = (V_2/V_1)^{1/3}$. - ☐ Reynolds number for impeller: $Re = \rho N D_i^2 / \mu$; fully turbulent when $Re > 10^4$. - ☐ Washout dilution: $D_c = \mu_{max} S_0/(K_s + S_0)$. - ☐ CSTR product concentration: $P = \tau X (\alpha \mu + \beta)$. - ☐ Impeller power (gassed): $P_g = N_p \rho N^3 D_i^5$; $N_p$ falls sharply with aeration (use gassed correction factor). - ☐ Sterilization regime: SIP at 121 °C for ≥ 20 min; CIP with NaOH (1–2 %) + acid rinse. - ☐ Most-cited industrial enzymes and their reactors: Subtilisin (STR, fed-batch), Glucoamylase (Airlift), Lactase (Packed bed immobilized), L-Asparaginase (Membrane). - ☐ Avoid catabolite repression by **limiting glucose** below threshold (typically 0.5 g/L).

topics.title: Purification of Extracted Enzymes: Centrifugation, Filtration, and Precipitation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT504 - ENZYME KINETICS & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Purification of Extracted Enzymes: Centrifugation, Filtration, and Precipitation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2 — Purification of Extracted Enzymes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBBBT504 — Module 2):**
> *Purification of extracted enzymes* is the downstream processing stage in which crude cell-free extracts (containing the desired enzyme alongside nucleic acids, polysaccharides, lipids, pigments, and contaminating proteins) are selectively separated, concentrated, and stabilized to yield a homogeneous, biologically active enzyme preparation suitable for kinetic characterization, structural studies, or industrial deployment.

The three foundational primary recovery / early-purification operations that KTU expects every B.Tech Biotechnology student to master are **Centrifugation**, **Filtration**, and **Precipitation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Stage Purification Philosophy (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Removal of insolubles** → Centrifugation & Filtration
> 2. **Concentration + initial fractionation** → Precipitation
> 3. **High-resolution polishing** → Chromatography & Electrophoresis (covered in Module 3)

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Gold-Panning" Intuition

Imagine you have a bucket of muddy river water flecked with tiny gold dust, sand grains, and floating leaves. To isolate the gold:

- You first **swirl the bucket rapidly** (centrifugation) — heavy sand sinks to the bottom.
- You then **pour the liquid through a fine sieve** (filtration) — leaves and pebbles are trapped.
- Finally, you **add a special chemical** that makes only the gold clump together and sink (precipitation).

Enzyme purification follows the exact same logic — except the "gold" is your target protein molecule, the "sand" is cellular debris, and the "special chemical" is ammonium sulfate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Metric Callout:**
> - Standard gravitational acceleration **g = 9.81 m·s⁻²**
> - Typical enzyme molecular weights range from **10 kDa to 500 kDa**
> - Industrial target purity = **>95 % homogeneity** (verified by SDS-PAGE)
> - Typical yield recovery per step = **60–90 %**

### 1.2 Formal Definitions of the Three Techniques

| Technique | KTU 2024 Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Centrifugation** | A mechanical separation process that uses centrifugal (pseudo-) force generated by high-speed rotation to sediment particles denser than the surrounding medium according to Stokes' law. |
| **Filtration** | A pressure- or vacuum-driven separation that retains particles above a defined size-cutoff on a porous membrane or depth matrix while permitting the solvent and smaller solutes to pass. |
| **Precipitation** | A solubility-based fractionation technique in which a target protein is selectively rendered insoluble (via salt, pH, organic solvent, or polymer), recovered by centrifugation/filtration, and redissolved. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CENTRIFUGATION — Principle & Theory

### 2.1.1 Operational Principle

A particle of mass **m** rotating at angular velocity **ω** at a radial distance **r** from the axis of rotation experiences an apparent centrifugal force. The sedimentation behavior is governed by a force balance between the centrifugal force and the opposing drag (frictional) force of the medium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why centrifugation works for enzymes:**
> Enzymes, ribosomes, membrane fragments, nucleic acids, and whole organelles differ markedly in **mass, density, and shape**. By tuning the rotor speed and run duration, sub-cellular components are fractionated into discrete pellets — enabling both **clarification** (removing debris) and **sub-cellular mapping**.

### 2.2 Types of Centrifugation (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Speed (× g) | Purpose | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-speed centrifugation** | 1,000 – 3,000 × g | Pellet whole cells, nuclei | Harvesting microbial biomass |
| **Differential centrifugation** | 10,000 – 20,000 × g | Mitochondria, lysosomes, peroxisomes | Sub-cellular fractionation |
| **High-speed centrifugation** | 30,000 – 80,000 × g | Microsomes, membrane vesicles | ER & Golgi isolation |
| **Ultracentrifugation** | > 100,000 × g | Ribosomes, viruses, proteins | Ribosomal subunit prep |
| **Density-gradient (rate-zonal)** | 50,000 – 100,000 × g | Separate by size & density | RNA–protein complexes |
| **Density-gradient (isopycnic)** | 100,000 – 600,000 × g | Separate purely by buoyant density | DNA, lipoproteins |

### 2.3 FILTRATION — Principle & Theory

Filtration separates particles by **size exclusion** through a porous medium. The driving force may be gravity, applied vacuum, positive pressure, or centrifugal force.

#### 2.3.1 Modes of Filtration

- **Dead-end filtration:** Liquid flows perpendicular to the membrane → cake formation (batch).
- **Cross-flow (tangential-flow) filtration:** Liquid sweeps parallel to the membrane → continuous, fouling-resistant (industrial standard for enzyme concentration).

#### 2.3.2 Membrane Classes (KTU Mandatory)

| Class | Nominal Pore / Cutoff | Removes | Retains Enzyme? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | 0.1 – 10 µm | Cells, cell debris | NO (enzyme passes) |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | 1 – 500 kDa | Proteins, enzymes | YES (concentrates enzyme) |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | 200 – 1,000 Da | Peptides, salts | Partial |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | < 200 Da | Water, ions | YES (water removal) |

### 2.4 PRECIPITATION — Principle & Theory

Precipitation exploits the **differential solubility** of proteins as a function of ionic strength, pH, temperature, and solvent composition. The target enzyme is driven out of solution and later redissolved in a small volume of appropriate buffer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why precipitation is so widely used in industry:**
> It is **cheap, scalable, gentle, and forgiving** — handling hundreds of liters with minimal equipment. It performs three jobs simultaneously: **purification, concentration, and stabilization** (the precipitated pellet is stable for months at 4 °C).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Pure — No Pipes Inside Tables)

$$
F_{\text{centrifugal}} \;=\; m \cdot \omega^{2} \cdot r
$$

$$
\text{RCF} \;=\; \frac{\omega^{2} \, r}{g} \;=\; 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \, n^{2} \, r
$$

$$
v_{\text{sed}} \;=\; \frac{\omega^{2} \, r}{f} \, \bigl(m_{\text{particle}} - m_{\text{displaced}}\bigr)
$$

$$
s \;=\; \frac{v_{\text{sed}}}{\omega^{2} \, r} \quad\quad\quad \text{(Svedberg coefficient)}
$$

$$
t \;=\; \frac{K_{\text{rotor}}}{s} \cdot \ln\!\Bigl(\frac{r_{\text{max}}}{r_{\text{min}}}\Bigr)
$$

$$
J \;=\; \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{\text{total}}} \quad\quad\quad \text{(Darcy's law for filtration flux)}
$$

$$
\log S \;=\; \log S_{0} \;-\; K_{s} \, I \quad\quad\quad \text{(Cohn salting-out equation)}
$$

$$
\%\,w/v \;=\; \frac{533 \, (S_{2} - S_{1})}{100 - 0.3 \, S_{2}} \quad\quad\quad \text{(Ammonium-sulfate addition table)}
$$

| Formula Symbol | Engineering Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{\text{centrifugal}}$ | Apparent centrifugal force | Newton (N) |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity | rad·s⁻¹ |
| $n$ | Rotor rotational speed | revolutions per minute (rpm) |
| $r$ | Radial distance from axis | metre (m) |
| $\text{RCF}$ | Relative Centrifugal Force ("× g") | dimensionless multiple of g |
| $v_{\text{sed}}$ | Terminal sedimentation velocity | m·s⁻¹ |
| $f$ | Frictional coefficient of particle | N·s·m⁻¹ |
| $s$ | Sedimentation (Svedberg) coefficient | svedberg (S) = 10⁻¹³ s |
| $K_{\text{rotor}}$ | Rotor geometry constant | dimensionless |
| $J$ | Volumetric flux through membrane | m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| $\Delta P$ | Trans-membrane pressure | Pascal (Pa) |
| $\mu$ | Viscosity of permeate | Pa·s |
| $R_{\text{total}}$ | Total membrane + cake resistance | m⁻¹ |
| $S$ | Protein solubility at ionic strength $I$ | mg·mL⁻¹ |
| $S_{0}$ | Theoretical solubility at zero ionic strength | mg·mL⁻¹ |
| $K_{s}$ | Salting-out constant (protein-specific) | M⁻¹ |
| $I$ | Ionic strength of solution | M (mol·L⁻¹) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> These equations underpin biopharmaceutical manufacturing (insulin, erythropoietin, monoclonal antibodies), vaccine purification (inactivated virus pellets), clinical diagnostics (centrifugal analyzers), food biotechnology (pectinase clarification of fruit juices), and environmental bioremediation (cell harvesting for biosensors).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Centrifugal Force and RCF

### Step 1 — Set up the kinematics

A particle in circular motion traces an arc at radius $r$ with linear (tangential) velocity:

$$
v \;=\; \omega \, r
$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity in rad·s⁻¹ and $r$ is the radial distance in metres.

### Step 2 — Derive the centrifugal pseudo-force

The centripetal acceleration required to keep the particle on its circular path is $a = v^{2}/r$. By Newton's second law, the reactive (perceived) centrifugal force on the particle of mass $m$ is:

$$
F_{\text{centrifugal}} \;=\; m \, a \;=\; m \, \frac{v^{2}}{r} \;=\; m \, \omega^{2} \, r
$$

This is the expression for the magnitude of the centrifugal (pseudo-) force acting radially outward on the particle.

### Step 3 — Convert to "× g" (Relative Centrifugal Force)

By definition, $\text{RCF}$ expresses the centrifugal force as a multiple of the standard gravitational force $m \, g$ acting on the particle at Earth's surface. Hence:

$$
\text{RCF} \;=\; \frac{F_{\text{centrifugal}}}{m \, g} \;=\; \frac{\omega^{2} \, r}{g}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert $\omega$ into revolutions per minute ($n$)

Since $\omega = 2\pi \, n / 60$ (one revolution = $2\pi$ rad, $n$ revolutions per minute = $n/60$ revolutions per second):

$$
\omega^{2} \;=\; \frac{4 \pi^{2} \, n^{2}}{3600} \;=\; 1.0966 \times 10^{-2} \, n^{2}
$$

Substituting back, with $g = 9.81$ m·s⁻² and $r$ expressed in centimetres (the standard lab convention, $r_{\text{cm}} = 100 \, r_{\text{m}}$):

$$
\text{RCF} \;=\; \frac{1.0966 \times 10^{-2} \, n^{2} \cdot r_{\text{cm}}/100}{9.81}
$$

$$
\text{RCF} \;=\; 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \, n^{2} \, r_{\text{cm}}
$$

This is the canonical "RCF calculator" formula. ∎

> [!TIP]
> **Worked conversion:** For a rotor of radius $r = 10$ cm spinning at $n = 10{,}000$ rpm:
> $\text{RCF} = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \times (10^{4})^{2} \times 10 = 11{,}180 \times g$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Svedberg Sedimentation Coefficient ($s$)

### Step 1 — Define terminal sedimentation velocity

In a centrifugal field, a particle reaches a constant terminal sedimentation velocity $v_{\text{sed}}$ when the centrifugal driving force equals the viscous drag force (Stokes' regime):

$$
m_{\text{eff}} \, \omega^{2} \, r \;=\; f \, v_{\text{sed}}
$$

where $m_{\text{eff}} = m_{\text{particle}} - m_{\text{displaced fluid}}$ (accounts for buoyancy by Archimedes' principle).

### Step 2 — Isolate the velocity

$$
v_{\text{sed}} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{eff}} \, \omega^{2} \, r}{f}
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Svedberg coefficient

The sedimentation coefficient $s$ is defined as the sedimentation velocity per unit centrifugal acceleration:

$$
s \;\equiv\; \frac{v_{\text{sed}}}{\omega^{2} \, r} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{eff}}}{f}
$$

It has units of seconds; to avoid unwieldy values, the conventional **svedberg** unit is used:

$$
1 \text{ S} \;\equiv\; 10^{-13} \text{ s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bigger picture:** Ribosomal subunits are named by their $s$-values — e.g., the *E. coli* 70S ribosome dissociates into a 50S large subunit + 30S small subunit (note: $s$-values are **not** additive because they depend on both mass and shape).

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Time Required for Complete Sedimentation

### Step 1 — Set up the differential equation

The particle moves radially outward at speed $dr/dt = v_{\text{sed}} = s \, \omega^{2} \, r$:

$$
\frac{dr}{dt} \;=\; s \, \omega^{2} \, r
$$

### Step 2 — Separate variables

$$
\frac{dr}{r} \;=\; s \, \omega^{2} \, dt
$$

### Step 3 — Integrate from meniscus ($r_{\text{min}}$) to pellet ($r_{\text{max}}$) and from $0$ to $t$

$$
\int_{r_{\text{min}}}^{r_{\text{max}}} \frac{dr}{r} \;=\; s \, \omega^{2} \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\ln\!\Bigl(\frac{r_{\text{max}}}{r_{\text{min}}}\Bigr) \;=\; s \, \omega^{2} \, t
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $t$

$$
t \;=\; \frac{1}{s \, \omega^{2}} \ln\!\Bigl(\frac{r_{\text{max}}}{r_{\text{min}}}\Bigr)
$$

For constant rotor geometry and a given run condition $\omega$, we group the geometry into $K_{\text{rotor}}$:

$$
t \;=\; \frac{K_{\text{rotor}}}{s} \cdot \ln\!\Bigl(\frac{r_{\text{max}}}{r_{\text{min}}}\Bigr)
$$

This is the expression used by rotor manufacturers (e.g., Beckman, Sorvall) to publish pelleting-time tables. ∎

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Cohn Salting-Out Equation

### Step 1 — Start from thermodynamic solubility

The logarithm of protein solubility decreases linearly with ionic strength at high salt concentrations (salting-out region):

$$
\log S \;=\; \log S_{0} \;-\; K_{s} \, I
$$

### Step 2 — Interpret the constants

- $S$ = protein solubility (mg·mL⁻¹) at ionic strength $I$.
- $S_{0}$ = extrapolated solubility at $I = 0$ (intrinsic).
- $K_{s}$ = **salting-out constant** — a positive, protein-specific number that quantifies how steeply solubility falls with ionic strength.

### Step 3 — Engineering application

By measuring $K_{s}$ for both the target enzyme and the main contaminant, one can choose an ammonium-sulfate concentration that **maximally precipitates the contaminant while keeping the target enzyme in solution** — the heart of "fractional precipitation". The standard graphic representation is a **solubility vs. ionic-strength plot** for each protein, where the target $I$ is chosen at the intersection of the two curves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenberg's additivity rule** (used implicitly in the ammonium-sulfate tables): the percent saturation of ammonium sulfate can be calculated from the change in solubility the salt produces — typically tabulated as the **"% w/v to add"** table using the equation:
> $\%\,w/v = \dfrac{533\,(S_{2} - S_{1})}{100 - 0.3\,S_{2}}$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Technique (KTU Exam Favourite)

| Property of Target Enzyme | Centrifugation | Filtration | Precipitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low abundance in crude extract** | ✗ Loses enzyme to supernatant | ✗ May pass membrane | ✓ Concentrates effectively |
| **Heat-labile / fragile** | ✗ Frictional heating risk | ✗ Shear at membrane | ✓ Cold ammonium-sulfate is gentle |
| **Membrane-associated** | ✓ Pellets with membranes | ✓ Retained on MF | ✗ Hard to solubilize selectively |
| **High molecular weight (>100 kDa)** | ✓ Pellets easily | ✓ Retained on UF | ✓ Precipitates at low salt |
| **Low molecular weight (<15 kDa)** | ✗ Will not pellet | ✗ Passes most UF | ✓ Best option |
| **Industrial scale-up (kL)** | ✓ Continuous-flow centrifuges | ✓ Cross-flow UF | ✓ Cheapest & most scalable |
| **Need to retain activity** | ✓ Fast, low-shear | ✓ Inert membranes | ✓ Rapid equilibration possible |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Workflow — Where Each Technique Fits


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]**

### Q1. Define centrifugation and explain the difference between differential and density-gradient centrifugation.

**Model Answer:**

**Centrifugation** is a separation technique in which a centrifugal (pseudo-) force, generated by rotating a sample at high angular velocity, sediments particles according to their mass, density, and shape. The sedimentation velocity is governed by the Svedberg relationship.

**Differential centrifugation** uses a *stepwise increase* in rotor speed. At each speed threshold, the largest/densest particles (cells → nuclei → mitochondria → microsomes → ribosomes) pellet first, while smaller particles remain in the supernatant. Particles are separated primarily by **size and sedimentation coefficient**.

**Density-gradient centrifugation** uses a pre-formed gradient of sucrose or cesium chloride. In **rate-zonal** mode, particles migrate at differential rates through a shallow gradient and are separated by **size + density**. In **isopycnic** mode, particles migrate to the point where their buoyant density equals the gradient density and are separated **purely by density**, independent of run time.

**Key distinction:** Differential = step-wise sedimentation by size; Density-gradient = continuous separation by size and/or equilibrium density in a pre-formed gradient.

---

### Q2. What is salting-out? Explain the principle of ammonium-sulfate precipitation of enzymes.

**Model Answer:**

**Salting-out** is the phenomenon in which increasing the ionic strength of a solution by adding a neutral salt (class I — e.g., ammonium sulfate, sodium sulfate) progressively **decreases the solubility** of a protein, eventually causing it to aggregate and precipitate.

**Mechanism (Hofmeister-series rationale):**
1. At low salt, small ions electrostatically shield protein charges → increased solubility (salting-**in**).
2. At high salt, ions compete with the protein for water of hydration → the protein loses its hydration shell.
3. Exposed hydrophobic patches on different protein molecules associate → aggregation → precipitation.

**Ammonium sulfate is preferred because:**
- High solubility in cold water (~4 M at 0 °C).
- Non-denaturing at the concentrations used.
- Inexpensive and non-corrosive.
- Density of the saturated solution is low enough to allow easy filtration/centrifugation.

The Cohn equation $\log S = \log S_{0} - K_{s} \, I$ quantitatively predicts the ionic strength at which a given protein precipitates; plotting $S$ vs. $I$ for both target and contaminant permits rational choice of a "cut" that fractionates the mixture.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Centrifugation Focus

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the relative centrifugal force (RCF) in terms of rotor speed (rpm, $n$) and rotor radius ($r$). Hence calculate the RCF generated by a rotor of radius 12 cm running at 14,000 rpm. **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**Derivation** (as derived in §3.1):
- Tangential velocity: $v = \omega r$
- Centripetal acceleration: $a = v^{2}/r = \omega^{2} r$
- Centrifugal pseudo-force: $F = m \, \omega^{2} r$
- RCF (relative to gravity): $\text{RCF} = \dfrac{F}{m \, g} = \dfrac{\omega^{2} r}{g}$
- Substitute $\omega = 2\pi n/60$: $\text{RCF} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} n^{2} r}{3600 \, g}$
- With $g = 981$ cm·s⁻² and $r$ in cm: $\text{RCF} = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \, n^{2} \, r$

**Numerical substitution:**
- Given: $n = 14{,}000$ rpm; $r = 12$ cm
- $\text{RCF} = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \times (14{,}000)^{2} \times 12$
- $(14{,}000)^{2} = 1.96 \times 10^{8}$
- $\text{RCF} = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.96 \times 10^{8} \times 12$
- $\text{RCF} = 1.118 \times 1.96 \times 12 \times 10^{3}$
- $\text{RCF} = 26{,}295 \times g$

**Answer:** $\boxed{\text{RCF} \approx 26{,}295 \times g}$

---

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a preparative ultracentrifuge. List four applications in enzyme technology. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
- A **high-vacuum chamber** (to eliminate air friction at > 40,000 rpm).
- A **drive motor** — usually an induction motor with frequency-controlled power supply.
- A **rotor** machined from a high-strength titanium or aluminium alloy, with precisely machined cavities for tubes (fixed-angle, swinging-bucket, vertical, or zonal rotors).
- A **refrigeration system** maintaining 0–4 °C to dissipate frictional heat and protect labile enzymes.
- An **optical system** (Schlieren, interferometric, or UV-absorption) for monitoring sedimentation in real time without stopping the run.
- A **control console** for speed, time, temperature, and vacuum.

**Working:**
1. Sample is loaded into a tube or zonal rotor.
2. Vacuum is established; rotor accelerates to the set speed.
3. Particles sediment according to $v_{\text{sed}} = s \, \omega^{2} r$, monitored optically.
4. After the run, fractions are collected by tube puncture, gradient displacement, or fractionation.
5. Pellets are recovered and resuspended; gradient fractions are analysed for the target enzyme.

**Four applications in enzyme technology:**
1. **Determination of molecular weight** of purified enzymes via sedimentation-equilibrium and sedimentation-velocity analysis.
2. **Purification of ribosome-bound enzymes** and viral particles.
3. **Isopycnic separation** of lipoprotein-associated enzymes (e.g., lecithin-cholesterol acyltransferase).
4. **Quality control** of recombinant enzyme batches for aggregate content.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Precipitation & Filtration Focus

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 model]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of isoelectric precipitation. How does it differ from salting-out precipitation? A protein has $\text{p}I = 5.4$. Suggest an appropriate buffer pH for its precipitation and justify. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of isoelectric precipitation:**
At the **isoelectric point (pI)**, the net charge on a protein is zero. With no electrostatic repulsion between molecules, protein–protein interactions (van der Waals + hydrophobic) dominate, leading to **aggregation and precipitation**. The solubility of a protein is at its **minimum** at the pI.

**Difference from salting-out precipitation:**

| Feature | Isoelectric Precipitation | Salting-Out |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Net charge = 0 | Dehydration by salt ions |
| Conditions required | pH adjustment (acid/base) | High ionic strength |
| Effect of salt | Often combined for yield | Essential |
| Selectivity | Moderate | High (fractional cuts possible) |
| Typical use | Initial crude prep | Bulk industrial recovery |

**Buffer pH suggestion:** **pH = 5.4 ± 0.1** (at or extremely close to the pI).
- Justification: at pH = pI, net charge is minimum → minimum solubility → maximum aggregation → maximum precipitation yield. The buffer should have a pKₐ within ±1 unit of 5.4 (e.g., **acetate buffer pKₐ = 4.76** or **MES buffer pKₐ = 6.15**) for adequate buffering capacity. Temperature should be lowered to 0–4 °C to prevent acid denaturation.

---

**(b)** Describe the principle and types of ultrafiltration. Derive the relationship between membrane flux and transmembrane pressure. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of ultrafiltration (UF):**
UF is a pressure-driven membrane process that **separates macromolecules (1 kDa – 500 kDa)** from solvents and low-MW solutes based on size. The membrane acts as a selective barrier; **transmembrane pressure (TMP)** forces solvent through the pores while macromolecules are retained and concentrated in the retentate.

**Types of UF configurations:**
- **Dead-end (stirred-cell) UF** — flow perpendicular to membrane; cake forms; used in lab.
- **Cross-flow (tangential-flow) UF** — feed flows parallel to the membrane; limits fouling; industrial standard.
- **Hollow-fibre UF** — large membrane area in a compact cartridge; very high throughput.
- **Spiral-wound UF** — flat membrane sheets wound around a permeate tube; common in pharma.
- **Vibratory / rotating-disk UF** — enhanced shear to combat fouling for viscous feeds.

**Derivation of flux–pressure relationship:**

By Darcy's law, the volumetric flux $J$ (m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹) through a porous membrane is proportional to the applied transmembrane pressure $\Delta P$ and inversely proportional to the viscosity $\mu$ of the permeate and the total hydraulic resistance $R_{\text{total}}$:

$$
J \;=\; \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{\text{total}}}
$$

For a clean membrane, $R_{\text{total}}$ reduces to the intrinsic membrane resistance $R_{m}$. As filtration proceeds, a concentration-polarization layer and a cake/gel layer build up on the membrane, contributing additional resistances $R_{c}$ (cake) and $R_{g}$ (gel):

$$
R_{\text{total}} \;=\; R_{m} \;+\; R_{c} \;+\; R_{g}
$$

At low TMP, $J$ increases linearly with $\Delta P$ (Darcy regime). Beyond a **critical flux** $J_{\text{crit}}$, the gel layer becomes fully formed; $J$ plateaus and additional pressure merely compresses the cake — a phenomenon called **flux decline**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **Do not** confuse the **Svedberg coefficient** (a physical property of a particle) with **rotor speed** or sedimentation time — these are not the same dimension.
> - ❌ **Do not** report RCF in "rpm" — it is dimensionless (× g); rpm is the input variable.
> - ❌ **Do not** state that "ammonium sulfate denatures proteins" — at the cold temperatures and short times of standard protocols, it is a **non-denaturing** precipitant.
> - ❌ **Do not** confuse **microfiltration** (removes cells, *retains* enzyme in filtrate) with **ultrafiltration** (concentrates enzyme in retentate) — this single sign error loses 2 marks.
> - ❌ **Do not** skip the **buoyancy correction** ($m_{\text{eff}} = m_{\text{particle}} - m_{\text{displaced fluid}}$) when deriving sedimentation velocity — it is mandatory for full credit.
> - ❌ **Do not** use **phosphate buffer** near pI = 5.4 — its pKₐ₂ = 7.2 is too far away; **acetate or MES** is the correct choice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centrifugation**
> - Pseudo-force: $F = m \, \omega^{2} \, r$; RCF formula: $1.118 \times 10^{-5} \, n^{2} \, r$ ($r$ in cm).
> - Sedimentation coefficient $s = m_{\text{eff}}/f$, unit = svedberg = $10^{-13}$ s.
> - Differential centrifugation separates by **size**; density-gradient by **size + density** (rate-zonal) or **density alone** (isopycnic).
> - Ultracentrifuge requires vacuum, refrigeration, optical monitoring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Filtration**
> - MF (0.1 – 10 µm) → removes cells; enzyme in filtrate.
> - UF (1 – 500 kDa) → concentrates enzyme in retentate.
> - Cross-flow (TFF) is the industrial standard; minimises fouling.
> - Flux law: $J = \Delta P / (\mu \, R_{\text{total}})$; critical flux limits productivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precipitation**
> - Salting-out: $\log S = \log S_{0} - K_{s} I$ — Cohn equation.
> - Isoelectric: minimum solubility at pI.
> - Organic solvents (cold acetone/ethanol): lower dielectric constant → aggregation.
> - PEG: volume-exclusion (polymer competes for water).
> - Ammonium sulfate is the **gold-standard** industrial precipitant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hooks for KTU Viva**
> - **C**entrifugation → **C**larifies debris
> - **F**iltration → **F**ractions by size
> - **P**recipitation → **P**ure concentrated pellet
> - Order in a real workflow: **C → F → P** (clarify, then sterilize, then concentrate).

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Key Numbers to Memorise**
> - $g = 9.81$ m·s⁻² (or 981 cm·s⁻²)
> - 1 S = $10^{-13}$ s
> - UF molecular-weight cutoffs: 1, 5, 10, 30, 50, 100 kDa (standard cassettes)
> - Ammonium sulfate saturation @ 0 °C ≈ 4.05 M
> - Typical fractional cut for first enzyme precipitation = **0 – 40 %** ammonium sulfate saturation
> - Typical fractional cut for second cut = **40 – 80 %** saturation

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT504 - ENZYME KINETICS & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Membrane Separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧪 MEMBRANE SEPARATION — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Membrane Separation** is a physical, pressure- or concentration-driven separation technique in which a **selectively permeable membrane** acts as a barrier to separate components of a mixture based on differences in **size, charge, shape, or chemical affinity**. It is a cornerstone of **Downstream Processing (DSP)** in enzyme technology, allowing concentration, purification, buffer exchange, and desalting of biomolecules without phase change or thermal degradation.

## 🎯 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> A **membrane** is a thin, semi-permeable barrier that permits the selective passage of certain species (permeate) while retaining others (retentate or concentrate) under the influence of a driving force. The driving force may be **pressure**, **concentration**, **electrical potential**, or **temperature gradient**.

## 💡 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sieve-in-the-Sand"

Imagine a beach where waves (the feed solution) carry a mixture of **pebbles (large cells/debris)**, **grains of sand (proteins/enzyme molecules)**, and **dissolved salt (ions)**. Now place a series of **wire-mesh screens** of decreasing pore size:

- The **coarsest mesh** stops the pebbles but lets sand and salt pass.
- The **finer mesh** stops the sand grains but lets salt pass.
- An **ultra-fine mesh** even stops the smallest sand, leaving only water and salts.

Each screen is a **membrane of a specific pore size**. The mixture passing through = **permeate**; what stays behind = **retentate**. Membrane separation works on exactly this principle — separating enzyme molecules from cells, debris, salts, and water without boiling, freezing, or chemically destroying anything.

## 🏷️ Key Terminology (KTU Board-Ready)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Feed** | The input solution entering the membrane module |
| **Permeate** | The fraction that passes *through* the membrane |
| **Retentate** | The fraction that is *retained* by the membrane |
| **Flux (J)** | Volume of permeate per unit membrane area per unit time |
| **Selectivity** | Ability of membrane to discriminate between species |
| **TMP** | Trans-Membrane Pressure — the pressure driving force |
| **MWCO** | Molecular Weight Cut-Off (in Daltons) |
| **Fouling** | Loss of performance due to deposition on membrane surface |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** Students must understand the *classification*, *driving forces*, *operational modes*, and *industrial applications* of microfiltration (MF), ultrafiltration (UF), nanofiltration (NF), reverse osmosis (RO), dialysis, and electrodialysis in the context of **enzyme recovery and purification**.

## ⚖️ Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Standard Pressure Unit:** **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa**
- **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO):** Expressed in **Daltons (Da)**
- **Flux Unit:** **L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (LMH)** or **m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹**
- **Viscosity of Water at 25 °C:** **η = 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧠 Fundamental Principles of Membrane Separation

The transport of solutes and solvents across a membrane is governed by the **solution-diffusion model**, the **pore-flow model**, or a combination depending on membrane type. Let us dissect the core operational logic step by step.

### 🔹 Step 1: Identifying the Driving Force

Membrane processes are classified first by their driving force:

1. **Pressure-driven:** MF, UF, NF, RO — increasing pressure = increasing flux.
2. **Concentration-driven:** Dialysis, Pervaporation — driven by chemical potential gradient.
3. **Electrical-potential-driven:** Electrodialysis — ions migrate under electric field.
4. **Temperature-driven:** Membrane Distillation — vapor pressure gradient.
5. **Hybrid:** Membrane chromatography, Membrane bioreactors.

### 🔹 Step 2: Pore-Size Classification (The Membrane Hierarchy)

```
Pore Size Decreases  →  Selectivity Increases  →  Pressure Required Increases
```

| Process | Pore Size | MWCO (Da) | Operating Pressure | Retains / Passes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | 0.1 – 10 µm | > 1,000,000 | 0.1 – 2 bar | Cells, debris / Proteins, salts |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | 1 – 100 nm | 1,000 – 500,000 | 1 – 10 bar | Proteins, enzymes / Salts, water |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | 0.5 – 5 nm | 200 – 1,000 | 5 – 35 bar | Sugars, divalent ions / Monovalent salts |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | < 0.5 nm | < 200 | 10 – 100 bar | All solutes / Pure water only |

### 🔹 Step 3: Modes of Operation

- **Dead-end (Static) Filtration:** Feed flows perpendicular to membrane; simple but high fouling.
- **Cross-flow (Tangential) Filtration:** Feed flows *parallel* to membrane surface, sweeping away deposited particles — preferred industrially for enzyme processing.

### 🔹 Step 4: Membrane Material Classification

- **Polymeric membranes:** Polysulfone (PS), Polyethersulfone (PES), Cellulose acetate, Polyamide.
- **Inorganic (ceramic) membranes:** Alumina (Al₂O₃), Zirconia (ZrO₂), Titania (TiO₂) — high thermal/chemical resistance.
- **Composite membranes:** Thin-film composite (TFC) with a dense skin on a porous support.

## 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following equations — they are **guaranteed high-yield** for KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{V_p}{A \cdot t}$ | **Flux** definition | m³/(m²·s) or LMH |
| 2 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$ | **Darcy's Law** for flux through membrane | m/s |
| 3 | $R_{obs} = R_m + R_f + R_c$ | Resistance-in-series model | m⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\Delta P = P_{feed} - P_{permeate}$ | Trans-membrane pressure (TMP) | Pa or bar |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot J}$ | Specific resistance | m⁻¹ |
| 6 | $C_R = C_F \left(\dfrac{V_F}{V_R}\right)$ | Concentration factor (mass balance) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $S = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | **Rejection coefficient** (Sieve/Retention) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $V_{permeate} = V_{feed} \cdot (1 - e^{-k t})$ | First-order permeate generation (declining flux) | m³ |
| 9 | $\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$ | **Van't Hoff** osmotic pressure | Pa |
| 10 | $\text{Rejection (\%)} = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100$ | Percent rejection for RO/NF | % |
| 11 | $\text{Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{V_R \cdot C_R}{V_F \cdot C_F} \times 100$ | Process yield | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** Rejection = 1 means the membrane is **completely impermeable** to the solute; Rejection = 0 means **freely permeable**.

## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Concentration of therapeutic enzymes (e.g., L-asparaginase, glucoamylase) using UF.
- **Dairy industry:** Whey protein concentration and lactose removal.
- **Water treatment:** RO desalination for pure water in fermentation media preparation.
- **Bioreactor downstream processing:** Cell harvesting via MF, enzyme concentration via UF.
- **Hemodialysis:** Artificial kidney removes urea from blood via UF/diffusion principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔢 Derivation 1: The Flux Equation (Darcy's Law for Membranes)

We start from **Darcy's empirical observation** that volumetric flow rate through a porous medium is proportional to the pressure drop and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity and the resistance offered by the medium.

**Step 1 — Define volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{V_p}{t}$$

where $V_p$ is the cumulative permeate volume in time $t$.

**Step 2 — Express flow rate per unit membrane area (flux):**

$$J = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{V_p}{A \cdot t}$$

**Step 3 — State Darcy's proportionality:**

$$J \propto \Delta P$$

$$J \propto \frac{1}{\mu}$$

$$J \propto \frac{1}{R_m}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into the canonical form:**

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

> **Logic Note:** $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity of the permeate (Pa·s); $R_m$ is the intrinsic hydraulic resistance of the clean membrane (m⁻¹); $\Delta P$ is the trans-membrane pressure (Pa). For *pure water*, this gives the *clean-water flux*, $J_w$.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 2: Resistance-in-Series Fouling Model

When fouling occurs (by cake formation, pore plugging, or adsorption), the total resistance becomes:

$$R_{obs} = R_m + R_f + R_c$$

Therefore the flux at any time becomes:

$$J(t) = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu (R_m + R_f + R_c)}$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$R_m \rightarrow \text{membrane (intrinsic)}$$

$$R_f \rightarrow \text{fouling (pore blocking + adsorption) — irreversible}$$

$$R_c \rightarrow \text{cake layer (reversible, removable by backwash)}$$

**Engineering Implication:** To restore flux, $R_c$ is removed by **backflushing**; $R_f$ can only be reduced by **chemical cleaning (CIP — Clean-in-Place)**. Routine operation therefore alternates between production cycles and CIP cycles.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 3: Rejection Coefficient and Sieve Mechanism

For a solute of concentration $C_f$ in the feed and $C_p$ in the permeate:

$$S = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}$$

If the membrane is perfectly retentive: $C_p = 0 \Rightarrow S = 1$ (100% rejection).

If the solute passes freely: $C_p = C_f \Rightarrow S = 0$ (0% rejection).

In **logarithmic form for RO**, real rejection relates to salt permeability:

$$S_{real} = \frac{J_v}{J_v + B}$$

where $J_v$ is the volumetric flux and $B$ is the salt permeability coefficient (m/s).

---

## 🔢 Derivation 4: Mass Balance for Batch Concentration

In a batch UF cell with feed volume $V_F$ and final retentate volume $V_R$, conservation of solute mass requires:

$$V_F \cdot C_F = V_R \cdot C_R + V_p \cdot C_p$$

If rejection is essentially 100% (i.e., $C_p \approx 0$, ideal for proteins):

$$V_R \cdot C_R = V_F \cdot C_F$$

$$C_R = C_F \cdot \left(\frac{V_F}{V_R}\right)$$

The **concentration factor (CF)** is therefore:

$$CF = \frac{V_F}{V_R} = \frac{C_R}{C_F}$$

> **Engineering Use:** A 10× concentration factor reduces downstream tankage volume by 90%, dramatically lowering capital cost in industrial enzyme manufacture.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 5: Osmotic Pressure and Reverse Osmosis

For an ideal dilute solution:

$$\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$$

When applying pressure $\Delta P$ across a semi-permeable membrane:

- If $\Delta P < \pi$ → **Osmosis** (water flows into concentrate).
- If $\Delta P > \pi$ → **Reverse Osmosis** (water flows from concentrate to dilute side).

**Net driving pressure for RO:**

$$\Delta P_{net} = \Delta P_{applied} - \Delta \pi$$

> **Logic Note:** As salt concentrates on the retentate side, $\Delta \pi$ rises, reducing the effective driving force. This is why RO flux declines over time in batch mode.

---

## 🔢 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice)

> **Problem:** A UF membrane of area **0.5 m²** produces **250 mL** of permeate in **30 minutes** from an enzyme feed. The TMP is **2.5 bar** and the viscosity of permeate is **1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**. Calculate the flux and the clean-membrane resistance.

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

$$V_p = 250 \text{ mL} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

$$t = 30 \text{ min} = 1800 \text{ s}$$

$$\Delta P = 2.5 \text{ bar} = 2.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute flux:**

$$J = \frac{V_p}{A \cdot t} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5 \times 1800}$$

$$J = 2.778 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert flux to LMH:**

$$J_{LMH} = 2.778 \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{3600}{10^{-3}} = 1.0 \text{ LMH}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R_m$ using Darcy's Law:**

$$R_m = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot J} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.778 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$R_m = 9.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Reject verification:**
This value is consistent with polymeric UF membranes in industrial use (typically $10^{12}$ – $10^{14}$ m⁻¹). ✓

---

## 📊 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Comparative Reasoning)

| Feature | MF | UF | NF | RO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pore size** | 0.1 – 10 µm | 1 – 100 nm | 0.5 – 5 nm | < 0.5 nm |
| **MWCO (Da)** | > 10⁶ | 10³ – 5×10⁵ | 200 – 10³ | < 200 |
| **Driving force** | 0.1–2 bar | 1–10 bar | 5–35 bar | 10–100 bar |
| **Retains** | Cells, bacteria | Proteins, enzymes | Sugars, divalent ions | All solutes |
| **Biotech use** | Cell harvesting | **Enzyme concentration** | Peptide purification | Water for media |
| **Fouling tendency** | High (cake) | Moderate | Low–moderate | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
        DE_F[Feed In] --> DE_M[(Vertical Membrane)]

    end

    end

    DE_M:::membrane

    CF_M:::membrane
    CF_P2:::perm
    CF_R2:::ret
```
