KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📚 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]** > **Define the term "Specific Activity" of an enzyme. Why is it considered a more reliable indicator of enzyme purity than total activity?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** *Specific Activity* is defined as the **number of enzyme units (U) per milligram of total protein**, expressed as: $$\text{Specific Activity} = \dfrac{\text{Total Activity (U)}}{\text{Total Protein (mg)}} \;\;[\text{U/mg}]$$ It is a more reliable purity indicator than total activity because **total activity** alone does not account for the presence of contaminating proteins. Two preparations may have identical total activity (e.g., 500 U) but vastly different purities — one may be 80% pure and the other 5% pure. Specific activity normalizes activity against total protein, so as purification progresses, contaminating proteins are removed and the specific activity **rises monotonically**, providing a direct numerical read-out of purity at every step. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** > **List any three components typically present in an enzyme extraction buffer and state the function of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Tris-HCl (50 mM, pH 7.5)** | Maintains constant physiological pH during extraction; prevents acid/alkaline denaturation | | **PMSF (1 mM)** | Irreversible inhibitor of serine proteases; prevents proteolytic degradation of target enzyme | | **β-Mercaptoethanol (1–5 mM)** | Reducing agent; maintains cysteine thiols in reduced state; prevents disulfide-mediated aggregation | | *EDTA (1–5 mM)* | *Chelates divalent cations; inhibits metalloproteases* | | *Glycerol (5–10%)* | *Cryoprotectant; stabilizes enzyme during low-temperature handling* | --- ## 📚 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice: A or B) --- ### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]* > **(a)** Describe, with a stepwise protocol, the **extraction of an industrially important enzyme** (e.g., amylase from *Bacillus subtilis* or papain from *Carica papaya*) from a suitable biological source. Include buffer composition, disruption method, and centrifugation conditions. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Outline the **principle, procedure, and calculation** involved in the **ammonium sulfate precipitation** step of enzyme purification. How would you determine the **optimal saturation cut** for your enzyme? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** **Selection of Source (1 mark):** For this PBL, *Bacillus subtilis* is selected as the source of **α-amylase** (EC 3.2.1.1) due to its high yield, GRAS (Generally Regarded As Safe) status, short fermentation cycle, and well-established downstream protocols. **Growth and Harvest (1 mark):** *B. subtilis* is cultured in a starch-containing liquid medium (e.g., **Luria-Bertani broth supplemented with 1% soluble starch**) at **37 °C, 180 rpm, for 18–24 h** until late-log phase. Cells are harvested by centrifugation at **8,000 × g, 4 °C, for 15 min**, and the pellet is washed twice with **50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.5)** to remove extracellular medium residues. **Extraction Buffer Composition (2 marks):** | Component | Concentration | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Tris-HCl | **50 mM, pH 7.5** | pH buffering | | NaCl | **100 mM** | Maintains ionic strength | | CaCl₂ | **5 mM** | α-Amylase is a calcium-dependent enzyme; stabilizes active site | | PMSF | **1 mM** | Serine protease inhibitor | | Lysozyme | **0.5 mg/mL** | Hydrolyzes bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan | | Glycerol | **10% (v/v)** | Stabilizes enzyme conformation | **Cell Disruption (2 marks):** The cell pellet is resuspended in **3 volumes of extraction buffer** (w/v) and subjected to **sonication on ice** using an ultrasonicator with the following parameters: - **Amplitude:** 30% - **Pulse cycle:** 30 s ON / 30 s OFF - **Total time:** 5 min (10 cycles) - **Temperature:** maintained below **8 °C** throughout **Centrifugation and Clarification (1 mark):** The lysate is centrifuged at **12,000 × g for 30 min at 4 °C**. The supernatant (cell-free extract, CFE) is carefully decanted, filtered through cheesecloth or a 0.45 µm membrane to remove lipid layers, and stored on ice for immediate assay or at **−20 °C for short-term storage** (≤ 1 week). --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Principle (2 marks):** At high concentrations, neutral salts (like ammonium sulfate) **compete with proteins for water molecules**, effectively dehydrating the protein surface. This reduces protein solvation and causes **"salting-out"** — precipitation of proteins. Different proteins precipitate at different salt concentrations depending on their surface hydrophobicity, allowing **fractional precipitation** and partial purification. **Procedure (2 marks):** 1. The CFE is brought to **0% saturation** (no salt) and a baseline is recorded. 2. Solid **(NH₄)₂SO₄** is added gradually with stirring on ice to reach **20% saturation** (using an established table). After 30 min equilibration, the suspension is centrifuged at **10,000 × g, 4 °C, 15 min**. The pellet (P1) is discarded; supernatant (S1) is retained. 3. The supernatant is brought to **40% saturation** → pellet (P2) discarded; supernatant (S2) retained. 4. The process is repeated at **60% and 80% saturation**, yielding pellets **P3 and P4**. 5. Each pellet is **redissolved in a small volume of assay buffer** and assayed for both **amylase activity** and **total protein**. **Determination of Optimal Cut (2 marks):** The optimal saturation range is identified by plotting two graphs: - **(i) Cumulative Activity vs. % Saturation** — identifies where the target enzyme precipitates. - **(ii) Specific Activity vs. % Saturation** — identifies where maximum purity is achieved. The cut that yields the **highest specific activity while retaining ≥ 70% of total activity** is selected as the optimal range (e.g., 40–70% for *B. subtilis* amylase, typically). **Calculation (1 mark):** For a target going from 0% → 70% saturation at 4 °C, the **Green & Hughes table** (or KTU lab manual table) indicates adding approximately **472 g of solid (NH₄)₂SO₄ per liter** of solution. The salt is added **slowly in small pinches over 30 min** to prevent local high concentration that may denature the enzyme. --- ### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]* > **(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the **principle and procedure of Ion-Exchange Chromatography (IEC)** for enzyme purification. Compare **cation-exchange** and **anion-exchange** media. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A student purified an enzyme and obtained the following data. Construct a **purification table** and comment on the **efficiency of each step**. **(7 marks)** | Step | Volume (mL) | Activity (U/mL) | Protein (mg/mL) | |---|---|---|---| | Crude extract | 100 | 10.0 | 4.0 | | (NH₄)₂SO₄ (40–70%) | 25 | 30.0 | 2.0 | | DEAE-Sepharose | 10 | 55.0 | 0.5 | | Sephadex G-75 | 5 | 80.0 | 0.2 | --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** **Principle (2 marks):** Ion-exchange chromatography separates proteins based on their **net surface charge** at a given pH. The stationary phase is a resin with covalently attached charged groups. Proteins of opposite charge bind to the resin, while same-charge proteins flow through. Bound proteins are eluted by **increasing ionic strength** (salt gradient) or **changing pH**. **Cation vs. Anion Exchange (2 marks):** | Feature | Cation Exchanger | Anion Exchanger | |---|---|---| | Functional group | **Carboxymethyl (CM)** or **Sulfopropyl (SP)** | **Diethylaminoethyl (DEAE)** or **Quaternary ammonium (Q)** | | Bound ions | Na⁺, H⁺ (exchangeable cations) | Cl⁻, OH⁻ (exchangeable anions) | | Binds proteins with | **Net positive charge** at working pH | **Net negative charge** at working pH | | Working pH | **Below the protein's pI** | **Above the protein's pI** | | Example resin | CM-Cellulose, SP-Sepharose | DEAE-Cellulose, Q-Sepharose | **Procedure (2 marks):** 1. **Equilibration:** The column is packed with resin and equilibrated with **low-salt buffer (20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.5)** until effluent pH and conductivity match the inlet buffer. 2. **Sample loading:** The dialyzed enzyme sample is loaded onto the column. Proteins of opposite charge bind; others flow through (collected as "flow-through"). 3. **Washing:** Unbound proteins are washed off with 2–3 column volumes of equilibration buffer. 4. **Elution:** A **linear salt gradient (0 → 500 mM NaCl)** is applied. Fractions of 1–2 mL are collected. 5. **Detection:** Each fraction is monitored at **A₂₈₀** (for protein) and assayed for **enzyme activity**. The activity peak is pooled. **Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the IEC elution profile schematic shown in Section 4 (Diagram 3) of these notes. --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **Construction of Purification Table (5 marks):** | Step | Volume (mL) | Activity (U/mL) | Total Activity (U) | Protein (mg/mL) | Total Protein (mg) | Specific Activity (U/mg) | Fold | Yield (%) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Crude extract | 100 | 10.0 | **1000** | 4.0 | **400** | **2.5** | **1.0** | **100** | | (NH₄)₂SO₄ (40–70%) | 25 | 30.0 | **750** | 2.0 | **50** | **15.0** | **6.0** | **75** | | DEAE-Sepharose | 10 | 55.0 | **550** | 0.5 | **5** | **110.0** | **44.0** | **55** | | Sephadex G-75 | 5 | 80.0 | **400** | 0.2 | **1** | **400.0** | **160.0** | **40** | **Calculations (1 mark shown — others by analogy):** *Crude extract:* - $\text{Total Activity} = 10.0 \times 100 = 1000 \;\text{U}$ - $\text{Total Protein} = 4.0 \times 100 = 400 \;\text{mg}$ - $\text{Specific Activity} = 1000/400 = 2.5 \;\text{U/mg}$ - $\text{Fold} = 2.5/2.5 = 1.0$; $\text{Yield} = 100\%$ *Ammonium sulfate:* - $\text{Specific Activity} = 750/50 = 15.0 \;\text{U/mg}$ - $\text{Fold} = 15.0/2.5 = 6.0$; $\text{Yield} = 750/1000 = 75\%$ **Commentary on Efficiency (2 marks):** 1. **Ammonium sulfate fractionation** provided a **6-fold purification** with **75% recovery** — a good first step, removing most bulk contaminants with minimal enzyme loss. 2. **DEAE-Sepharose (anion exchange)** gave a **further 7.3-fold purification** (44 ÷ 6) and **25% loss in yield** — this is the **workhorse step** of the protocol, providing the largest absolute jump in specific activity. 3. **Sephadex G-75 (gel filtration)** provided a final **3.6-fold purification** with **27% yield loss** — a polishing step that removes trace contaminants and aggregates, evidenced by the very high final specific activity (**400 U/mg**) and the single-band appearance on SDS-PAGE (not shown in data but expected). The overall process is **efficient**: a 160-fold purification with 40% yield is an **excellent outcome** for a 3-step protocol. The drop in yield is compensated by the dramatic increase in purity, making this protocol publication-quality. > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Calculation error in total values** — forgetting to multiply by volume is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks in the purification table. > 2. **Confusing fold and yield** — Fold can be > 1 (it always increases), but yield can NEVER exceed 100%. If your calculation gives 110%, you have an arithmetic error. > 3. **Omitting units** — A₂₈₀ is **dimensionless**; protein concentration is **mg/mL**; activity is **U/mL**; specific activity is **U/mg**. Always write units. > 4. **Forgetting the buffer pH and salt concentration** when describing IEC — the choice of buffer and pH relative to the protein's pI is the **conceptual core** of the answer. > 5. **No mention of "orthogonal methods"** — IEC and gel filtration separate by **different principles** (charge vs. size). The examiner expects you to state this explicitly to justify why both steps are needed. > 6. **Skipping the SDS-PAGE step** — even if you do not have the gel image, mention that the final step's purity is confirmed by a **single band on 12% SDS-PAGE**, corresponding to the expected molecular weight. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Your Exam!** - ✅ **Specific Activity = Total Activity / Total Protein (U/mg)** — the universal purity index. - ✅ **Purification Fold = SA(final) / SA(initial)** — must be ≥ 1 at every step. - ✅ **Yield (%) = Total Activity (final) / Total Activity (initial) × 100** — must be ≤ 100% always. - ✅ **Michaelis-Menten equation:** $V_0 = \dfrac{V_{\max} [S]}{K_m + [S]}$. - ✅ **Lineweaver-Burk equation:** $\dfrac{1}{V_0} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{\max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{\max}}$. - ✅ **Km = substrate concentration at V₀ = Vmax/2** — directly readable from hyperbolic MM plot. - ✅ **kcat = Vmax / [E]T** — turnover number; units s⁻¹. - ✅ **Catalytic efficiency = kcat / Km** — the best indicator of enzymatic perfection (theoretical max ≈ 10⁸–10⁹ M⁻¹s⁻¹, diffusion limit). - ✅ **Extraction buffer essentials:** Tris-HCl (pH 7.5, 50 mM), NaCl (100–150 mM), PMSF (1 mM), β-ME (1–5 mM), glycerol (5–10%). - ✅ **Sonication parameters:** 20 kHz, 30% amplitude, 5 × 30 s pulses, ice-cooled. - ✅ **Standard centrifugation for CFE:** 10,000–12,000 × g, 20–30 min, 4 °C. - ✅ **Ammonium sulfate:** added on ice, slowly, with stirring; fractional cuts at 0–20, 20–40, 40–60, 60–80%. - ✅ **Dialysis:** 10–14 kDa MWCO membrane, ≥ 4 buffer changes over 12–24 h, 4 °C. - ✅ **IEC:** Use DEAE (anion) for proteins with pI < 7; use CM (cation) for proteins with pI > 7. - ✅ **Gel filtration:** Sephadex G-25 (desalting), G-75 (10–50 kDa proteins), G-100 (40–100 kDa), G-200 (70–200 kDa). - ✅ **SDS-PAGE:** 12% Laemmli gel is the KTU default; molecular weight determined by comparison to standard ladder. - ✅ **Optimum pH study:** use at least **5–6 different buffers** spanning pH 3.0–10.0. - ✅ **Optimum temperature study:** assay at **4, 25, 37, 50, 60, 70, 80 °C** for 5–10 min. - ✅ **Always include a "Discussion" section** addressing yield loss, fold gain, and possible causes of enzyme inactivation (proteolysis, oxidation, temperature, pH, surface adsorption). - ✅ **Reference standard protocols** — Palmer's "Enzymes: Biochemistry, Biotechnology, Clinical Chemistry", Worthington Enzyme Manual, or Scopes' "Protein Purification: Principles and Practice" (no need to cite editions). > [!TIP] > **🎓 Final Pro Tip for KTU 2024:** > When asked about PBL in the viva, confidently explain the **trade-off** between **purification fold** and **yield** — this single insight demonstrates mature understanding of bioprocess engineering. Examiners reward students who think like engineers, not just lab technicians.

topics.title: Project Implementation Guidelines: Enzyme Selection, Literature Review, Bioreactor Design, Purification, Characterization, and Industrial Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT504 - ENZYME KINETICS & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Implementation Guidelines: Enzyme Selection, Literature Review, Bioreactor Design, Purification, Characterization, and Industrial Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Implementation Guidelines in Enzyme Technology** refer to the systematic, sequential framework of methodologies, decision matrices, and standard operating procedures (SOPs) that govern the execution of an enzyme-based research/industrial project — from the initial **enzyme selection** and **literature review**, through **bioreactor design** and **downstream purification**, to final **characterization** and deployment in **industrial applications**.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education), this module equips the student with the *project competency* to:
- Identify a target enzyme based on industrial/economic rationale,
- Design a fermentation/production strategy,
- Devise a multi-step purification protocol,
- Biochemically characterize the final product, and
- Map the enzyme to a viable commercial application.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pharmaceutical Factory" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of executing an enzyme project exactly like a chef building a five-star recipe from scratch.

| Project Stage | Chef Analogy | Real Biotechnology Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Enzyme Selection** | Choosing the right main ingredient (chicken vs paneer) | Choosing source organism & enzyme (e.g., *Bacillus lipase* vs *Candida lipase*) |
| **Literature Review** | Reading cookbooks, YouTube tutorials, food blogs | Reviewing PubMed, Scopus, patents, KTU journals |
| **Bioreactor Design** | Choosing the cooking vessel (pressure cooker, tandoor, sous-vide) | Selecting CSTR, PFR, Airlift, Membrane, or Packed-bed reactor |
| **Purification** | Filtering impurities, separating oil from gravy | Ammonium sulfate precipitation → chromatography → HPLC |
| **Characterization** | Taste-testing for salt, spice, texture balance | SDS-PAGE, Km, Vmax, pH optima, thermal stability |
| **Industrial Applications** | Serving the dish in a restaurant | Detergents, pharma, biofuel, food, textile, paper |

> **Key Engineering Metric:** The **overall project success** is quantified by a triad — *Yield (η), Specific Activity (SA), and Purification Fold (PF)* — the **"Holy Trinity"** of enzyme project evaluation.

### 1.3 The Six Pillars of an Enzyme Project

1. **Enzyme Selection** — Choice of biocatalyst & source.
2. **Literature Review (LR)** — Evidence-based scoping.
3. **Bioreactor Design** — Process engineering & scale-up.
4. **Purification (DSP)** — Downstream processing.
5. **Characterization** — Biochemical fingerprinting.
6. **Industrial Applications** — Commercial translation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillar 1 — Enzyme Selection (The Strategic Foundation)

**Operational Logic Steps:**
- **Step 1: Define the Target Reaction.** What substrate conversion is desired? (e.g., starch → glucose, oils → fatty acids, lactose → galactose + glucose).
- **Step 2: Identify Candidate Enzymes.** Use databases — *BRENDA*, *ExPASy/UniProt*, *CAZy*, *NCBI* — to shortlist 3–5 enzymes.
- **Step 3: Source Selection.** Decide between **microbial** (recombinant *E. coli*, *Pichia pastoris*, *Bacillus subtilis*), **plant** (papain, bromelain), or **animal** (trypsin, pepsin) origins.
- **Step 4: Application-Environment Matching.** Choose mesophilic (20–45 °C), thermophilic (50–80 °C), or psychrophilic (<15 °C) enzymes.
- **Step 5: Regulatory & Safety Profiling.** GRAS status, allergenicity, toxin profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Criteria Matrix (used by KTU evaluators):**
> - Specificity (regio-/stereo-/chemo-selectivity)
> - Turnover number (k_cat)
> - Stability (thermal, pH, solvent)
> - Cost of production (COP)
> - Availability of expression host

### 2.2 Pillar 2 — Literature Review (The Evidence Compass)

- **Step 1:** Frame research question using **PICO** (Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome).
- **Step 2:** Search databases: **PubMed, Scopus, Google Scholar, Web of Science, ScienceDirect, patents (WIPO, USPTO, Espacenet)**.
- **Step 3:** Filter using Boolean operators: `(enzyme OR biocatalyst) AND (kinetics OR immobilization) NOT (review)`.
- **Step 4:** Synthesize a **PRISMA-style flow diagram** (Identification → Screening → Eligibility → Inclusion).
- **Step 5:** Identify the **research gap** — this becomes your project's *novelty statement*.

### 2.3 Pillar 3 — Bioreactor Design (The Engineering Core)

**Reactor Choice Decision Tree:**

| Reactor Type | Best For | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch / CSTR** | Whole-cell catalysis, high flexibility | $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ |
| **Fed-Batch** | Catabolite repression avoidance | Mass balance with $F_{in}$ |
| **PFR (Plug Flow)** | Continuous substrate conversion | $\tau = \int \frac{dS}{-r_S}$ |
| **Airlift / Bubble Column** | Low-shear, aerobic | $k_La = f(P/V, u_g)$ |
| **Packed-Bed (PBR)** | Immobilized enzyme | Thiele modulus $\phi$ |
| **Membrane / Hollow-Fiber** | Cofactor recycling, product removal | Permeate flux $J$ |

> **Design Parameters (Bold Standard Metrics):**
> - **Dilution Rate (D) = F/V** with units of **h⁻¹**
> - **Residence Time (τ) = V/F** with units of **h**
> - **Volumetric Productivity (Qp) = P/(V·t)** with units of **g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹**

### 2.4 Pillar 4 — Purification / Downstream Processing (DSP)

**Step-Wise Purification Ladder:**

1. **Cell Disruption:** Sonication / French press / Lysozyme / Bead mill.
2. **Clarification:** Centrifugation (10,000 × g) + 0.45 µm filtration.
3. **Concentration:** Ultrafiltration (UF) with **10 kDa MWCO** membranes.
4. **Capture (Precipitation):** Ammonium sulfate (0–80 % saturation) or acetone.
5. **Intermediate Purification:** Ion Exchange Chromatography (IEX), Hydrophobic Interaction Chromatography (HIC).
6. **Polishing:** Gel Filtration (GF) / Size Exclusion Chromatography (SEC), Affinity Chromatography (AC), HPLC (RP-HPLC).

### 2.5 Pillar 5 — Characterization

**Biochemical Fingerprint Parameters:**
- **Molecular Weight (MW):** SDS-PAGE, MALDI-TOF, Size-exclusion HPLC.
- **Optimum pH & Optimum Temperature:** Activity vs. pH/T curve.
- **Kinetic Parameters:** $K_m$, $V_{max}$, $k_{cat}$, $k_{cat}/K_m$.
- **Stability:** Half-life ($t_{1/2}$), $T_m$ (melting temperature), $T_{50}^{60}$ (temperature at which 50% activity is lost in 60 min).
- **Inhibition Profile:** Competitive, non-competitive, uncompetitive, mixed.
- **Storage Stability:** Lyophilization, cryoprotectants (glycerol, trehalose).

### 2.6 Pillar 6 — Industrial Applications

| Sector | Enzyme Example | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Detergents** | Subtilisin, lipase | Stain removal |
| **Food** | α-Amylase, pectinase, lactase | Starch processing, juice clarification, milk treatment |
| **Pharma** | Penicillin acylase, lipase | 6-APA production, chiral synthesis |
| **Biofuel** | Cellulase, xylanase | Lignocellulose → fermentable sugars |
| **Textile** | Laccase, peroxidase | Bleaching, denim finishing |
| **Paper & Pulp** | Xylanase | Bio-bleaching |
| **Cosmetics** | Superoxide dismutase (SOD) | Anti-aging |
| **Diagnostics** | Glucose oxidase, HRP | Biosensors |

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **every formula** the KTU 2024 examiner expects you to know for a project on enzyme technology.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Michaelis–Menten | $v = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | $v$: reaction rate; $V_{max}$: max rate; $K_m$: half-saturation constant |
| 2 | Lineweaver–Burk | $\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$ | Linearized form |
| 3 | Eadie–Hofstee | $v = -K_m \cdot \dfrac{v}{[S]} + V_{max}$ | Alternative plot |
| 4 | Specific Activity (SA) | $SA = \dfrac{\text{Total Activity (U)}}{\text{Total Protein (mg)}}$ | U/mg |
| 5 | Purification Fold (PF) | $PF = \dfrac{SA_{step}}{SA_{crude}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Yield (η) | $\eta = \dfrac{Activity_{step}}{Activity_{crude}} \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | Turnover Number | $k_{cat} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{[E]_t}$ | s⁻¹ |
| 8 | Catalytic Efficiency | $\dfrac{k_{cat}}{K_m}$ | M⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 9 | Batch Dilution Rate | $D = \dfrac{F}{V}$ | h⁻¹ |
| 10 | CSTR Substrate Conversion | $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Arrhenius (Inactivation) | $\ln k_d = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | rate constant $k_d$ |
| 12 | Thermal Half-life | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_d}$ | min or h |
| 13 | Immobilized Efficiency | $\eta_{immob} = \dfrac{Activity_{immob}}{Activity_{free}} \times 100$ | % |
| 14 | Thiele Modulus | $\phi = L \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{D_{eff}}}$ | for PBR |
| 15 | Volumetric Productivity | $Q_p = \dfrac{P}{V \cdot t}$ | g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 16 | Bradford Binding | $A_{595} = f(\text{protein})$ | absorbance |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Purification Fold** with **Yield**. Purification Fold = *purity increase*; Yield = *activity recovered*. They are **independent metrics** — a step can have high fold but low yield (over-purification loss).

---

### 2.8 Real-World Utility of This System

- **Pharma:** $Q_p$ & SA drive *cost per gram* calculations for biologics manufacturing.
- **Food industry:** $t_{1/2}$ at pasteurization temperatures determines shelf-life of enzyme additives.
- **Biofuel:** Thiele modulus optimization for packed-bed cellulase reactors determines *capital expenditure (CAPEX)*.
- **Detergents:** $k_{cat}/K_m$ at 30–60 °C dictates cleaning efficiency in washing machines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Michaelis–Menten Equation (Steady-State)

Starting from the rapid-equilibrium model:

$$
E + S \;\underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; ES \;\overset{k_{2}}{\rightarrow}\; E + P
$$

**Step 1:** Rate of product formation:

$$
v = k_{2} [ES]
$$

**Step 2:** At steady state, $\dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} = 0$:

$$
k_{1}[E][S] = (k_{-1} + k_{2})[ES]
$$

**Step 3:** Total enzyme conservation: $[E]_t = [E] + [ES]$

**Step 4:** Substituting and solving for $[ES]$:

$$
[ES] = \dfrac{[E]_t [S]}{K_m + [S]}, \quad \text{where } K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_{2}}{k_{1}}
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$
\boxed{v = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lineweaver–Burk Linearization

**Step 1:** Take reciprocal of Michaelis–Menten:

$$
\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m + [S]}{V_{max}[S]} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}[S]} + \dfrac{[S]}{V_{max}[S]}
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify:

$$
\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}
$$

This is a linear plot with:

- **y-intercept** = $1/V_{max}$
- **x-intercept** = $-1/K_m$
- **slope** = $K_m/V_{max}$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Activity, Purification Fold & Yield Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Favourite Question:** A table is given, and you are asked to compute the **Purification Fold** and **Yield** for each step.

**Worked Example (Numerical Derivation):**

Suppose your crude extract has the following data:
- Total Volume = **100 mL**
- Total Protein = **500 mg**
- Total Activity = **1000 U**

**Step 1 — Calculate Initial Specific Activity:**

$$
SA_{crude} = \dfrac{1000\ \text{U}}{500\ \text{mg}} = 2.0\ \text{U/mg}
$$

**Step 2 — After Ammonium Sulfate Precipitation:**
- Total Volume = 25 mL
- Total Protein = 250 mg
- Total Activity = 800 U

$$
SA_{salt} = \dfrac{800}{250} = 3.2\ \text{U/mg}
$$

$$
PF_{salt} = \dfrac{3.2}{2.0} = 1.6
$$

$$
\eta_{salt} = \dfrac{800}{1000} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Step 3 — After Ion Exchange Chromatography (IEX):**
- Total Volume = 10 mL
- Total Protein = 50 mg
- Total Activity = 500 U

$$
SA_{IEX} = \dfrac{500}{50} = 10.0\ \text{U/mg}
$$

$$
PF_{IEX} = \dfrac{10.0}{2.0} = 5.0
$$

$$
\eta_{IEX} = \dfrac{500}{1000} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**Step 4 — After Gel Filtration (Final Step):**
- Total Volume = 5 mL
- Total Protein = 10 mg
- Total Activity = 300 U

$$
SA_{GF} = \dfrac{300}{10} = 30.0\ \text{U/mg}
$$

$$
PF_{GF} = \dfrac{30.0}{2.0} = 15.0
$$

$$
\eta_{GF} = \dfrac{300}{1000} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

**Final Compiled Purification Table:**

| Step | Volume (mL) | Protein (mg) | Activity (U) | SA (U/mg) | PF (×) | Yield (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Crude | 100 | 500 | 1000 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 100 |
| (NH₄)₂SO₄ | 25 | 250 | 800 | 3.2 | 1.6 | 80 |
| IEX | 10 | 50 | 500 | 10.0 | 5.0 | 50 |
| Gel Filtration | 5 | 10 | 300 | 30.0 | 15.0 | 30 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The enzyme has been purified **15-fold** with a recovery of **30 %** — a typical acceptable result for a 4-step lab-scale purification.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bioreactor Volumetric Productivity

Consider a CSTR with:
- Substrate inlet: $S_0$ (g/L)
- Substrate outlet: $S$ (g/L)
- Product: $P$ (g/L)
- Reactor volume: $V$ (L)
- Flow rate: $F$ (L/h)

**Step 1:** Mass balance on substrate at steady state:

$$
F S_0 = F S + V r_S
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for residence time:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{V}{F} = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{r_S} = \dfrac{X \cdot S_0}{r_S}
$$

**Step 3:** Volumetric Productivity:

$$
Q_p = \dfrac{P \cdot F}{V} = \dfrac{P}{\tau} \quad [\text{g·L}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1}]
$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Arrhenius-Based Thermal Inactivation

Enzyme denaturation follows first-order kinetics:

$$
\dfrac{d[E]}{dt} = -k_d [E]
$$

**Step 1:** Integrate:

$$
[E] = [E]_0 \, e^{-k_d t}
$$

**Step 2:** Arrhenius dependence of $k_d$:

$$
k_d = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}
$$

**Step 3:** Taking log:

$$
\ln k_d = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}
$$

**Step 4:** Thermal half-life:

$$
t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_d} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_d}
$$

> **Real-World Insight:** A thermophilic enzyme from *Thermus aquaticus* (Taq polymerase) has $t_{1/2} \approx 40$ min at 95 °C — the molecular basis for **PCR**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flowchart — End-to-End Project Implementation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Conception] --> B[Step 1 Enzyme Selection]
    B --> C[Step 2 Literature Review PICO and PRISMA]
    C --> D[Step 3a Strain Engineering or Source]
    D --> E[Step 3b Bioreactor Design CSTR PFR Airlift PBR]
    E --> F[Step 3c Fermentation and Production]
    F --> G[Step 4a Cell Disruption]
    G --> H[Step 4b Clarification and Concentration]
    H --> I[Step 4c Ammonium Sulfate Precipitation]
    I --> J[Step 4d Chromatography IEX HIC GF AC HPLC]
    J --> K[Step 5a Purity Check SDS PAGE]
    K --> L[Step 5b Kinetic Characterization Km Vmax kcat]
    L --> M[Step 5c Stability Profile pH T solvents]
    M --> N[Step 6a Validation and Scale Up]
    N --> O[Step 6b Industrial Application Detergents Food Pharma Biofuel]
    O --> P[Step 6c Economic and Life Cycle Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Bioreactor Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Enzyme Type] --> B{Free or Immobilized}
    B -->|Free| C[Batch CSTR]
    B -->|Immobilized| D[Packed Bed Reactor PBR]
    C --> E[Aerobic or Anaerobic]
    E -->|Aerobic| F[Airlift Bubble Column]
    E -->|Anaerobic| G[Stirred Tank Bioreactor]
    D --> H[Continuous PFR]
    F --> I[Downstream Processing]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Purification and Characterization]
```

### 4.3 Multi-Step Purification Strategy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Extract] --> B[Step 1 Cell Disruption Sonication]
    B --> C[Step 2 Centrifugation 10000 xg]
    C --> D[Step 3 Ammonium Sulfate 80 percent Saturation]
    D --> E[Step 4 Dialysis or UF 10 kDa MWCO]
    E --> F[Step 5 Ion Exchange Chromatography]
    F --> G[Step 6 Gel Filtration or Affinity Chromatography]
    G --> H[Step 7 RP HPLC Polishing]
    H --> I[Homogeneous Enzyme]
    I --> J[Purity Check SDS PAGE]
    J --> K[Activity Assay]
    K --> L[Specific Activity Fold and Yield Calculation]
```

### 4.4 Characterization Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Purified Enzyme] --> B[SDS PAGE MW determination]
    A --> C[MALDI TOF Mass Spectrometry]
    A --> D[Zymography Activity Gel]
    A --> E[Optimum pH Assay]
    A --> F[Optimum Temperature Assay]
    A --> G[Lineweaver Burk Plot Km Vmax]
    A --> H[Thermal Stability T50 60]
    A --> I[Storage Stability Lyophilization Cryoprotectants]
    B --> J[Final QC Certificate]
    C --> J
    D --> J
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

### 4.5 Industrial Application Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Industrial Enzyme Classes] --> B[Hydrolases]
    A --> C[Oxidoreductases]
    A --> D[Transferases]
    A --> E[Lyases]
    A --> F[Isomerases]
    A --> G[Ligases]
    B --> H[Detergents Food Textile]
    C --> I[Biosensors Bioremediation]
    D --> J[Pharma Synthesis]
    E --> K[Food Industry]
    F --> L[Sugar Isomerization]
    G --> M[DNA Ligation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*"Define Specific Activity, Purification Fold, and Yield. Why are all three metrics essential in evaluating a purification protocol?"*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Specific Activity (SA):** Total enzyme activity (U) divided by total protein (mg), expressed in **U/mg**. Indicates *purity* per unit mass of protein. *(1 mark)*
- **Purification Fold (PF):** Ratio of SA at a given purification step to SA of the crude extract. Indicates the *degree of purification* achieved. *(1 mark)*
- **Yield (η):** Percentage of total activity recovered after each step relative to the crude. Indicates *recovery efficiency*. *(1 mark)*

> **Why all three?** A single metric is misleading — high PF with very low yield means the enzyme is *pure but wasted*. Conversely, high yield with low PF means *abundant but impure*. The triad ensures a *balanced* protocol.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"What is the role of a Lineweaver–Burk plot in enzyme characterization? List the two intercepts and the slope."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- The **Lineweaver–Burk plot** is a double-reciprocal linearization of the Michaelis–Menten equation, used to determine $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ graphically. *(1 mark)*
- **Y-intercept** = $1/V_{max}$ → used to calculate $V_{max}$. *(1 mark)*
- **X-intercept** = $-1/K_m$ → used to calculate $K_m$.
- **Slope** = $K_m/V_{max}$. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 4 Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — 14-Mark Comprehensive Long-Answer**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

> *"You have been awarded a B.Tech project to develop a microbial lipase for the detergent industry. As a project investigator, outline the complete project implementation plan, covering: (a) Enzyme selection and literature review strategy (7 marks), and (b) Bioreactor design, downstream purification, and characterization protocol (7 marks)."*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Enzyme Selection & Literature Review Strategy [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Industrial Need Definition (1 mark)**
- Detergent lipases must work at: **pH 9–11**, temperature **30–60 °C**, in the presence of surfactants, oxidizing agents (perborate), and chelators (EDTA). This narrows the choice to **alkaline, thermostable, surfactant-tolerant lipases**.

**Step 2: Database Mining (1 mark)**
- Search **BRENDA** for *alkaline lipase*, *detergent lipase*. Shortlist candidates from *Bacillus licheniformis*, *Bacillus subtilis*, *Pseudomonas alcaligenes*, and *Candida antarctica* (Lipase B).

**Step 3: Selection Criteria Matrix (2 marks)**
- High $k_{cat}/K_m$ at pH 10
- $t_{1/2} \geq 30$ min at 60 °C
- GRAS-safe expression host
- Patent landscape — *Candida antarctica* Lipase B (CALB) is heavily patented → choose *Bacillus subtilis* lipase for novelty.

**Step 4: Literature Review (PICO) (1.5 marks)**
- **P:** Microbial lipases
- **I:** Alkaline thermostable variants
- **C:** Wild-type vs engineered/immobilized
- **O:** $K_m$, $T_{50}^{60}$, washing performance
- Databases: PubMed, Scopus, Espacenet (patents).
- PRISMA: Identify → Screen → Eligibility → Include (final ~40 papers).

**Step 5: Research Gap (1.5 marks)**
- Few reports on a *B. subtilis* lipase simultaneously tolerant to **SDS + peroxide + EDTA at pH 10, 50 °C** — define this as the novelty.

---

**(b) Bioreactor Design, Purification, and Characterization [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Bioreactor Choice (1.5 marks)**
- Use a **7 L stirred-tank bioreactor (STR)** with **fed-batch mode** to avoid catabolite repression by glucose.
- Operating conditions: 30 °C, pH 7.0 (for production), DO > 30 %, agitation 200–500 rpm.
- For immobilized enzyme at later stage → **Packed-Bed Reactor (PBR)** with $L$ = 10 cm, $\phi$ < 1 (no diffusion limitation).

**Step 2: Downstream Purification Strategy (2.5 marks)**
1. **Cell disruption** by French press (15,000 psi) or lysozyme.
2. **Ammonium sulfate precipitation** (60 % saturation) — capture.
3. **Dialysis / UF (10 kDa MWCO)** — desalting.
4. **IEX (DEAE-Sepharose, pH 8.0, NaCl gradient 0–0.5 M)** — intermediate.
5. **Gel filtration (Sephadex G-100)** — polishing.
6. **RP-HPLC (C18 column, ACN gradient)** — final QC.

**Step 3: Characterization Protocol (2 marks)**
- **Purity:** SDS-PAGE — single band at expected MW (~45 kDa for *B. subtilis* lipase).
- **Kinetic parameters:** $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ via **Lineweaver–Burk** with $p$-NPP substrate.
- **Optima:** pH 10, 50 °C; pH stability 7–11; thermal stability $T_{50}^{60} \approx 45$ min.
- **Detergent compatibility:** residual activity ≥ 80 % in 1 % SDS, 1 % Tween-80, 5 mM EDTA.
- **Washing test:** Standard EMPA-101 cotton swatches; reflectometric comparison vs. commercial lipase (Lipolase®).

**Step 4: Validation & Industrial Translation (1 mark)**
- Scale-up to 50 L, then 500 L pilot STR.
- Immobilize on **Accurel MP-1000** for use in PBR.
- Techno-economic analysis: target selling price < ₹ 800 / kg enzyme.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

> *"A crude cell extract has a total volume of 50 mL, total protein of 200 mg, and total lipase activity of 2000 U. After ammonium sulfate precipitation, the volume is 20 mL, protein is 80 mg, and activity is 1600 U. After ion-exchange chromatography, the volume is 5 mL, protein is 8 mg, and activity is 800 U.*
>
> *(a) Construct the purification table and calculate Specific Activity, Purification Fold, and Yield at each step (7 marks).*
>
> *(b) With the purified enzyme, a Lineweaver–Burk plot yields a y-intercept of 0.001 min/μmol and a slope of 0.05 min. Calculate the $K_m$ and $V_{max}$. Discuss the suitability of this enzyme for industrial-scale transesterification in biodiesel production (7 marks)."*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Purification Table and Metric Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Crude Extract**
- $SA = 2000 / 200 = 10\ \text{U/mg}$
- $PF = 1.0$, $\eta = 100\ \%$ *(1 mark)*

**Step 2: Ammonium Sulfate**
- $SA = 1600 / 80 = 20\ \text{U/mg}$
- $PF = 20/10 = 2.0$
- $\eta = (1600/2000) \times 100 = 80\ \%$ *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 3: IEX**
- $SA = 800 / 8 = 100\ \text{U/mg}$
- $PF = 100/10 = 10.0$
- $\eta = (800/2000) \times 100 = 40\ \%$ *(1.5 marks)*

**Final Purification Table:** *(1 mark)*

| Step | Volume (mL) | Protein (mg) | Activity (U) | SA (U/mg) | PF (×) | Yield (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Crude | 50 | 200 | 2000 | 10 | 1.0 | 100 |
| (NH₄)₂SO₄ | 20 | 80 | 1600 | 20 | 2.0 | 80 |
| IEX | 5 | 8 | 800 | 100 | 10.0 | 40 |

**Conclusion of Part (a):** The enzyme has been purified **10-fold** with **40 % overall recovery** — an acceptable laboratory-scale outcome. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) $K_m$, $V_{max}$ Calculation and Industrial Suitability [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Recall Lineweaver–Burk Equation (1 mark)**

$$
\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}
$$

**Step 2: Identify Intercepts and Slope (1 mark)**
- Y-intercept = $1/V_{max} = 0.001$ min/μmol
- Slope = $K_m / V_{max} = 0.05$ min

**Step 3: Calculate $V_{max}$ (1.5 marks)**

$$
V_{max} = \dfrac{1}{0.001} = 1000\ \mu\text{mol·min}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate $K_m$ (1.5 marks)**

$$
K_m = \text{slope} \times V_{max} = 0.05 \times 1000 = 50\ \mu\text{M}
$$

**Step 5: Industrial Suitability Discussion (2 marks)**

- $K_m = 50\ \mu\text{M}$ indicates a **moderate to high affinity** for the substrate. Acceptable for biodiesel feedstocks where oil concentrations are typically **> 1 M triglyceride equivalents** — substrate >> $K_m$, so $v \approx V_{max}$ in industrial reactors.
- $V_{max} = 1000\ \mu\text{mol·min}^{-1}$ is a reasonable lab-scale activity.
- **Biodiesel suitability requires additionally:**
  - Thermostability at 40–60 °C.
  - Tolerance to methanol (excess methanol inactivates lipases → use stepwise methanol addition).
  - Reusability: immobilize on **acrylic resin / Accurel MP-1000** to enable ≥ 100 reuse cycles.
  - pH optima near neutral, since FFAs are produced.
- **Verdict:** The kinetic profile is *promising*, but the enzyme must additionally pass **methanol tolerance**, **reusability**, and **scale-up** tests to be industrially viable.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Module 4 Long-Answer Questions:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Purification Fold" with "Yield."** They are *independent* — a step can have high fold but low yield. Many students lose 2–3 marks by not maintaining this distinction in tables.
> 2. **Units in Lineweaver–Burk:** Y-intercept = 1/V_max, NOT V_max. Writing V_max on the y-intercept loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Reactor choice rationale:** Always justify your reactor choice with **kinetics + mode of operation** (free vs immobilized, aerobic vs anaerobic, batch vs continuous). Bare naming without justification is penalized.
> 4. **PICO/PRISMA** in literature review — examiners expect a *structured* protocol, not generic "I read papers." Failure to use PICO/Boolean operators loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **In bioreactor design** — forgetting to mention **DO (dissolved oxygen), pH control, and antifoam** costs marks.
> 6. **In characterization** — missing **storage stability** (lyophilization, cryoprotectants) is a frequent omission.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- ☐ **Project Workflow:** Enzyme Selection → Literature Review → Bioreactor Design → Purification → Characterization → Industrial Application.
- ☐ **Selection Criteria:** Specificity, $k_{cat}$, stability, cost, GRAS status, patent freedom.
- ☐ **LR Tools:** PICO framework, PRISMA flow, Boolean operators, databases (PubMed/Scopus/Espacenet).
- ☐ **Reactor Selection:** Batch / CSTR (free enzyme), PBR (immobilized), Airlift (low shear, aerobic), Membrane (cofactor recycling).
- ☐ **Design Equations:** $D = F/V$, $\tau = V/F$, $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$, $Q_p = P/(V \cdot t)$.
- ☐ **Purification Metrics:** SA = Activity/Protein; PF = SA_step/SA_crude; η = (Activity_step/Activity_crude) × 100.
- ☐ **Chromatography Hierarchy:** Capture (salt) → Intermediate (IEX/HIC) → Polishing (GF/AC/HPLC).
- ☐ **Characterization Toolkit:** SDS-PAGE (MW), Lineweaver–Burk (Km, Vmax), pH/T optima curves, Arrhenius ($E_a$, $t_{1/2}$).
- ☐ **$k_{cat}/K_m$** = catalytic efficiency, the "true" enzyme performance metric.
- ☐ **Industrial Mapping:** Hydrolases → detergents/food/textile; Oxidoreductases → biosensors/bioremediation; Transferases → pharma.
- ☐ **Biodiesel lipase critical params:** methanol tolerance, reusability (immobilization), $T_{50}^{60}$, $K_m$ for triglyceride substrate.
- ☐ **Detergent enzyme critical params:** pH 9–11 optimum, surfactant (SDS) tolerance, oxidant (perborate) tolerance, thermal stability 50–60 °C.
- ☐ **Always use the triad (SA, PF, η)** together to evaluate a purification step.
- ☐ **Always mention PBR Thiele modulus** when designing immobilized enzyme reactors.
- ☐ **Always cite $k_{cat}/K_m$** when comparing two enzymes for the same application.
